finding: Automation/'no human' language is 8.1x more common in negative Trustpilot reviews and pervasive in Reddit AI talk (lift 1.05) -- the signature of forced automation.
design_principle: Make the human hand-off one click away; never trap users in a bot loop. Label AI as AI and state its limits up front.
framework: Algorithm aversion (Dietvorst et al. 2015); TAM perceived usefulness (Davis 1989)

finding: Errors / 'doesn't work' / hallucination is the cleanest driver: 1.8x (Reddit) and 6.9x (Trustpilot) more common in negative text.
design_principle: Show confidence and sources; let users correct the agent; fail loudly, not silently.

finding: Slow / unresolved support drives the negative class in both sources (lift 1.2 Reddit / 1.8 Trustpilot); resolution speed beats politeness.
design_principle: Optimise the agent for first-contact resolution and a transparent wait/status, not chat length.
framework: UTAUT effort expectancy & facilitating conditions (Venkatesh et al. 2003)

finding: Persona/companionship language is emotionally loaded and tilts slightly negative in both sources (lift ~1.5) -- strong delight when it works, strong frustration when the persona disappoints.
design_principle: Match persona warmth to role (personable for AI-native companions, low-key for transactional add-ons) and keep the persona stable -- abrupt changes break trust.

finding: Opaque fees / refunds dominate negative rental reviews (5.4x on Trustpilot) but are NOT an AI-native frustration (lift 0.7 on Reddit).
design_principle: Surface all-in price and refund rules before commitment; let the agent proactively explain charges.

finding: Trust / scam / verification language marks negative rental reviews (4.9x on Trustpilot), less so on Reddit (0.9x).
design_principle: Foreground verification, security and data-use cues; let the agent cite its safeguards.
